note: CONTROL — Figure 1 should be reported, nothing extra

items.caption: Our method identifies the extrapolation optimum using only a small fraction of the original scaling-law fitting budget. On 1r&bsz, the predicted optimum over learning rate and batch size for a 1B-parameter LLM trained on 100B tokens reaches the low-loss region within 1% of the budget, as shown by the trajectory on the test-loss heatmap (left). The selected training configurations used for fitting are shown on the right in a 3D view of the design space, colored by training cost (6ND), illustrating that accurate extrapolation can be achieved from a sparse, low-cost subset of the full configuration space.
items.summary: Two plots showing a heatmap of test loss over learning rate and batch size (left) and a 3D scatter plot of design space configurations (right), illustrating extrapolation optimum identification within a small budget fraction.

items.caption: Table 1: Task statistics for the scaling-law benchmark. Each task contains a collection of scaling-law instances for evaluating budget-aware sequential design on target-region extrapolation.
items.summary: Summarizes task statistics for the scaling-law benchmark including laws, parameters, training/test sizes, targets, and costs.

note: MISS — should recover Figure 2 (4-panel line plot) and Table 2

items.caption: Mean target-region $R^2$ as a function of consumed budget on the benchmark. Our method reaches the strongest overall budget--accuracy trade-off and approaches the full-data reference using only a small fraction of the total experimental cost.
items.summary: Mean target-region R² across benchmark tasks as a function of consumed budget, showing method performance relative to baselines.

items.caption: Task-level breakdown of target-region $R^2$ under different budget levels. Each cell reports the mean and standard deviation aggregated over all scaling-law instances within the corresponding task. Higher is better. Budgets are 1%, 5%, 10% for most tasks, and 20%, 35%, 50% for domain and sparsity.
items.summary: Task-level R² scores with means and standard deviations across budget levels and settings.

items.caption: Figure 3: Parameter-space visualization for one lr&bsz scaling law (s1_5) after fitting on the cheapest 12% of training points from 2048 initializations. We embed the fitted parameters with t-SNE and color each solution by its MSE on the selected points (left) or on the held-out test region (right). Multiple separated clusters indicate many local optima, while the mismatch between the two colorings shows that low error on the observed low-cost points does not reliably imply low error on the high-cost extrapolation region. Our method achieves 0.71 averaged test R² within 5% budget compared with 0.57 for V-opt and 0.16 for D-opt.
items.summary: Parameter-space visualization showing MSE gaps for selected-set and test regions with clusters indicating local optima and extrapolation challenges.

items.caption: Ablation study of the acquisition function. We remove either $\Delta V_{\text{inter}}$ or $\Delta V_{\text{intra}}$ from the acquisition score and report the target-region $R^2$ at different budget levels. Each cell shows the mean and standard deviation aggregated over all scaling-law instances. Higher is better. Budgets are 1\%, 5\%, 10\% for most tasks, and 20\%, 35\%, 50\% for domain and sparsity.
items.summary: Table showing ablation results for acquisition function components with R² values across different budget levels and settings.

items.caption: Collected scaling laws grouped by task.
items.summary: The table lists collected scaling laws grouped by task, including their ID, parametric form, number of parameters, and test R² values.
